claimed_contribution: The paper distinguishes and operationalizes two coexisting meanings of background music in advertising: embodied hedonic meaning and referential semantic meaning.
It develops and tests a resource-based account of when each music meaning affects product perceptions, based on processing intensity and the resource demands of verbal ad format.
It demonstrates a way to disentangle embodied versus referential influence by pairing music executions whose two meanings imply opposite inferences on targeted perception measures.
context: The studies are set in muted-background-music radio advertisements for consumer services, specifically a travel agency and a bookstore. Participants listened to target and filler radio ads and then reported perceptions, thoughts, and recall.
design_and_data: The paper reports two between-subjects laboratory experiments using professionally recorded radio ads with low-volume background music. Experiment 1 used 77 students, measured processing intensity via need for cognition, and crossed ad message format (lecture vs drama) with energetic vs sedate executions of the same unfamiliar melody in a travel-agency ad. Experiment 2 used 109 undergraduates, manipulated processing intensity, crossed message format with background music condition (classical vs novel soulful vs no music) in a bookstore ad, and included pretests validating message demand and music meanings. Dependent measures were product perception indexes plus coded thought-listing and recall responses.
main_findings: Low-NFC or nonintensive processors showed no reliable effects of muted background music on target product perceptions and generated more thoughts about peripheral executional aspects of the verbal message.
Among intensive processors, when the message was in a less demanding lecture format, perceptions and thought/recall responses reflected the music's referential meaning.
Among intensive processors, when the message was in a more demanding drama format, perceptions shifted toward the music's embodied hedonic meaning, favoring the more stimulating music execution.
one_line_summary: Across two radio-ad experiments, intensive processors used music's referential meaning under low message-demand conditions but relied on embodied hedonic meaning under high message-demand conditions, whereas nonintensive processors were largely insensitive to muted background music.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior work that examined music-induced mood and music-induced associations separately by modeling embodied and referential meanings as distinct but coexisting influences in advertising. It also identifies resource-based boundary conditions for when each meaning is used in product judgment.
research_gap: Prior research suggested that background music can evoke either affective responses or semantic associations, but it did not clearly distinguish these meanings in one framework or show which one consumers use when evaluating advertised products. The paper addresses this by testing when each meaning affects judgments under different processing and message-demand conditions.
research_question: Which meaning of background ad music, embodied hedonic meaning or referential semantic meaning, influences perceptions of advertised products? When do consumers rely on one meaning, the other, or neither, as a function of processing intensity and the resource demands of ad message format?
